title: Neo-Tokyo Drive, Vol. 1
tonic_progression: Dm -> Am -> Em -> Bm (Dorian) -> F#m -> C#m (Phrygian) -> G#m -> D#m (Phrygian-dominant flavor) -> A#m / Bbm -> Fm -> Cm -> Dm
narrative_arc: Twelve tracks read as a single vertical descent through Neo-Tokyo at night, ignition to ground floor. Tracks 1-3 are the ignition and the entry to the highway: a single neon-red bike kicks over in a parking deck high above the city (1), threads onto an elevated expressway between skyscrapers stacked with vertical signage (2), and merges into the bike-pack at full traffic density along the upper deck (3). Tracks 4-7 are the chase arc through descending tiers of the city: a rival cuts in and pursuit begins on the upper deck at high traffic (4), the chase spills off the expressway and through an arcade district where signage walls strobe orange and violet across the road (5), drops into the lower-tier night market alley where the bikes weave between food stalls and hanging cables (6), and bottoms out at the album's peak BPM in a derelict tunnel under the overpass where the sodium lamps flicker red (7). Tracks 8-11 are the descent into the underground proper: bikes leave the surface streets and drop down a service ramp into a concrete substructure of pipes and steam (8), continue deeper through a flooded maintenance corridor with reflected neon on standing water (9), arrive at an empty cathedral-like reactor hall buried at the city's base where the only light is the cool violet glow of the cooling pool (10), and begin the climb back up a single freight elevator shaft toward street level (11). Track 12 closes the descent at street level after dawn-fade: the bike is parked, the rider is gone, the signage above has dimmed to its daytime palette, and the album's opening motif plays one final time on FM bell over an unhurried analog brass pad — the same idea but resigned, the violence of the night absorbed into the city's normal frequency. The descent rings closed on the same tonic that opened it (D minor), but the listener arrives at it from below rather than from above.

recurring_motifs.motif: Neon ignition motif — a four-note descending FM bell phrase voiced 5-b3-2-1 (sol-me-re-do in minor) with a long ring-out on the final note, the album's red-violet ignition signature
recurring_motifs.transformation: Track 1 plays it whole and clean — a single voice on FM bell over an analog brass pad, the bike kicking over at altitude. Track 5 plays it strobed: the same four pitches but retriggered at 16th-note rate through a hard-gated tremolo, mirroring the strobing signage walls of the arcade district. Track 7 plays it inverted (ascending 1-2-b3-5) in the chase peak, the motif climbing as the BPM ceiling is reached — the only ascending appearance on the album. Track 12 plays it whole again at the original four-note descending shape but voiced on FM bell one octave lower with a long analog brass pad sustain underneath, resigned and slow, the ignition gesture re-read as a shutdown gesture.

recurring_motifs.motif: Vertical-signage stack — an eight-note analog-brass figure built from two interlocking four-note phrases stacked an octave apart, the lower phrase playing on the downbeats and the upper phrase entering on the and-of-1, creating a literal vertical-stack listening shape that mirrors the album's vertical-descent visual logic
recurring_motifs.transformation: Track 3 introduces it at full traffic density on the upper deck with both phrases unfiltered, sidechain pumping hard, the listener locking into the bike-pack. Track 6 brings it down a register and routes the upper phrase through a slap-delay so the two phrases smear into each other — the night market alley where the signage is closer to the road and harder to read clearly. Track 10 plays only the lower phrase, very slowly, on a single analog brass voice with deep convolution reverb — the cathedral-like reactor hall where the city's signage is no longer audible, only the memory of its rhythm.

recurring_motifs.motif: Phrygian descent cell — a three-note chromatic-falling cell (b2 -> 1 -> 7) used as the album's threshold gesture, articulated on layered analog brass with a hard sidechain on the b2 strike
recurring_motifs.transformation: Track 4 introduces it as the cut-in moment when the rival closes — a single articulation at the chorus midpoint, the b2 strike landing on the bar-line. Track 8 makes it the structural backbone of the underground-entry section: the cell articulated every 4 bars as the bikes ramp down into the substructure, each articulation timed to a visual depth-step into the concrete. Track 11 returns it once as a single articulation at the moment the freight elevator latches at ground level — mirroring track 4's single articulation but reversed in meaning (track 4 was the cut-in that started the chase; track 11 is the latch that ends the descent).

tracks.title: Ignition Deck

sections.name: intro
sections.instrumentation: [Intro - low analog brass pad on D, no rhythm, distant traffic-hum noise floor, single FM bell ping every 4 bars on the tonic]

sections.name: verse
sections.instrumentation: [Verse - + NEON IGNITION MOTIF whole on FM bell played once per 8 bars, hard-square sub bass on root, soft kick on 1 only, no snare yet]

sections.name: chorus
sections.instrumentation: [Chorus - + soft analog brass stab on beat 1 of each bar, gated reverb snare on 2 and 4, sidechain on brass pad pumping with the kick, neon-ignition motif repeats on every 4-bar phrase]

sections.name: bridge
sections.instrumentation: [Bridge - drums drop, just analog brass pad and FM bell motif, listener takes a breath at altitude]

sections.name: outro
sections.instrumentation: [Outro - pad sustains D, FM bell motif plays whole one final time, fades to traffic-hum floor]

tracks.musicgen_prompt: synthwave 104 BPM D minor, low analog brass pad, FM bell playing a four-note descending phrase with long ring-out, hard-square sub bass on root, soft kick on beat one, gated reverb snare on 2 and 4, sidechain pumping on the brass pad, distant traffic-hum noise floor, sense of a single neon-red bike kicking over in a parking deck high above a Neo-Tokyo skyline at night, anticipatory red-violet, instrumental, no vocals
tracks.cover_prompt: akira 1988 neo-tokyo album cover, view from inside a high-rise parking deck open to the night cityscape, single neon-red sport motorcycle parked centered in the foreground with its tail-light glowing crimson, the cityscape behind through the open deck-edge showing dense vertical stacks of red and violet kanji signage on skyscraper faces extending down out of frame and up out of frame, a single horizontal magenta neon strip running along the parking deck ceiling above the bike, deep black night sky behind the buildings with no stars, no people, no faces, no text, anime-cyberpunk-1988 aesthetic, red-orange-violet palette, no warm sunset colors, no cyan-blue, 1024x1024
tracks.composer_notes: Sets the tonic (D minor) and the album's signature voice pairing of FM bell over analog brass pad. Both Sunset Vol. 1 and Tron Vol. 1 opened on A minor; this album deliberately starts a perfect fourth higher on D minor to establish its own harmonic home base distinct from the prior two volumes. NEON IGNITION MOTIF introduced whole and clean on FM bell over analog brass pad — this is the album's thesis statement. No drums until bar 32 to give the brass pad and FM bell room to establish the voice palette before the rhythmic engine arrives. Outro ends on D tonic (not the dominant) — the descent begins on stillness, not motion. Track 2 takes the V (A) of D minor and reroots it as its own tonic for the entry to the expressway.

tracks.title: Onramp Above the City

sections.name: intro

sections.name: verse

sections.name: chorus
sections.instrumentation: [Chorus - + analog brass lead playing a four-bar phrase in Am, layered analog brass pads underneath, FM bell counter-line on the resolution of each phrase, hard sidechain pumping]

sections.name: verse2
sections.instrumentation: [Verse - drop the brass lead, the muted-pluck arpeggio foregrounds with a second voice an octave up]

sections.name: chorus2
sections.instrumentation: [Chorus - full mix, brass lead doubled in octaves, FM bell counter doubled, drums at full]

sections.name: outro
sections.instrumentation: [Outro - filter close on E (V of Am), brass pad sustains, kick fades]

tracks.musicgen_prompt: synthwave 108 BPM A minor, filtered analog brass pad, chugging muted-pluck arpeggio root-third-fifth, hard kick on every beat, gated reverb snare on 2 and 4, hard-square sub bass, analog brass lead playing a four-bar phrase, FM bell counter-melody on phrase resolutions, hard sidechain pumping, sense of a neon-red bike entering an elevated expressway between Neo-Tokyo skyscrapers, rolling red-amber, instrumental, no vocals
tracks.cover_prompt: akira 1988 neo-tokyo album cover, low-angle view of an elevated expressway onramp at night curving up between two enormous skyscrapers stacked floor-to-roof with red and orange kanji signage, a single neon-red sport motorcycle on the onramp in mid-frame leaning into the curve, neon orange tail-lights of other bikes already on the expressway ahead, the road surface glistening violet from reflected signage, deep black sky between the buildings, no people, no faces, no text, anime-cyberpunk-1988 aesthetic, red-orange-violet palette, no warm sunset, no cyan-blue, 1024x1024
tracks.key_relationship_to_prior: Up a perfect fifth from D minor — the V chord of D minor (A major) is reread as A minor, a parallel-minor pivot that keeps the album strictly inside the minor mode while still feeling like a directional lift. Sunset's ascent used minor/major alternation; Tron's used cycle-of-fifths inside minor. Neo-Tokyo uses cycle-of-fifths strictly inside minor, like Tron, but with the V-as-minor pivot trick added so each step feels slightly more concealed and urban.
tracks.tempo_relationship_to_prior: +4 BPM. First step of a tight mechanical accelerando.
tracks.composer_notes: First true cruiser. The muted-pluck arpeggio is the album's locked-in rhythmic engine — keep it dry and tight, no reverb on the pluck itself. Analog brass lead must read as a Yamaha CS-80 / Roland D-50 brass voice, not a saw-stack — this is the album's tonal differentiator from Tron's PWM-saw signature. Sidechain pumping on the analog brass pad is the album's central groove gesture. Outro E (V of Am) pivots cleanly to Em of track 3 — the V is reread as the new tonic, same trick as the 1->2 pivot, the descent ladder advancing one mechanical click.

sections.name: chorus

sections.name: breakdown
sections.instrumentation: [Breakdown - kick drops, just the two interlocking brass phrases plus pad, no drums, the listener locks into the vertical stack]

sections.name: chorus2
sections.instrumentation: [Chorus - full kit returns, both phrases foregrounded, brass pad behind, FM bell counter doubled]

sections.name: outro
sections.instrumentation: [Outro - filter close on B (V of Em), brass pad sustains, kick fades]

tracks.musicgen_prompt: synthwave 112 BPM E minor, tight sixteenth-note muted-pluck arpeggio, hard kick on every beat, gated reverb snare on 2 and 4, hard-square sub bass, two interlocking analog brass phrases stacked an octave apart with the lower phrase on downbeats and the upper phrase on the and-of-1, hard sidechain pumping on both phrases, FM bell counter-melody, sense of a neon-red bike at full traffic density along an elevated expressway between skyscrapers stacked floor-to-roof with vertical signage, dense red-orange-violet, instrumental, no vocals
tracks.cover_prompt: akira 1988 neo-tokyo album cover, view from behind a single neon-red sport motorcycle riding inside dense traffic on an elevated expressway at night, skyscrapers on both sides of the road stacked with vertical strips of red and violet kanji signage reaching up out of frame and down out of frame, multiple orange tail-lights of other bikes streaking ahead, the road surface reflecting the signage in violet, motion blur on the buildings passing, no people visible (the riders are in helmets and visors), no faces, no text, anime-cyberpunk-1988 aesthetic, red-orange-violet palette, no warm sunset, no cyan-blue, 1024x1024
tracks.key_relationship_to_prior: Up a perfect fifth from Am to Em — second step of the cycle-of-fifths ascending ladder strictly inside the minor mode, the same architectural step Tron took from track 1 to track 2. Neo-Tokyo's pacing is tighter though: this album takes the ladder one step further (1->2->3->4->5->6) before pivoting into modal interchange at track 7, where Tron pivoted at track 4.
tracks.composer_notes: VERTICAL-SIGNAGE STACK MOTIF introduced. The two interlocking analog brass phrases are the album's central architectural gesture — the listener should hear the literal vertical stack in the voicing. Lower phrase on downbeats, upper phrase entering on the and-of-1, octave apart, both phrases the same four-note shape. The breakdown at bar 41 is where the listener should first hear the stack without drums obscuring it — give it air. 270s long because the motif introduction deserves the extra room. Outro B (V of Em) pivots to Bm of track 4 — the V reread as new tonic continues, but track 4 introduces Dorian color which shifts the album's harmonic flavor for the chase arc.

tracks.title: Cut-In

sections.name: intro
sections.instrumentation: [Intro - analog brass pad swell on B, reverse cymbal up to bar 1, single low FM bell hit, no rhythm yet]

sections.name: verse
sections.instrumentation: [Verse - + kick on every beat, hard-square sub, tight 16th-note muted-pluck arpeggio on B-D-F#, gated reverb snare, sidechain medium]

sections.name: pre-chorus
sections.instrumentation: [Pre-chorus - snare rolls, white-noise riser, filter open all the way, brass pad swell]

sections.name: chorus
sections.instrumentation: [Chorus - + layered analog brass lead playing a four-bar phrase in B Dorian (note the G# raised-6th in the melody), FM bell counter-line on phrase resolutions, PHRYGIAN DESCENT CELL articulated once at bar 8 (C natural -> B -> A on layered analog brass with hard sidechain on the C strike — the cut-in moment), full kit, hard sidechain]

sections.name: break
sections.instrumentation: [Break - drums drop, just the muted-pluck arpeggio plus brass pad, the listener hears the rival closing]

sections.name: chorus2
sections.instrumentation: [Chorus - full return, brass lead doubled in octaves, phrygian descent cell articulated once more at bar 8 with even harder sidechain]

sections.name: outro
sections.instrumentation: [Outro - filter close on F# (V of Bm), brass pad sustains, kick fades]

tracks.musicgen_prompt: synthwave 114 BPM B minor with Dorian raised-sixth color (G sharp in the melody), tight sixteenth-note muted-pluck arpeggio, hard kick on every beat, gated reverb snare on 2 and 4, hard-square sub bass, layered analog brass lead playing a four-bar phrase, a three-note chromatic descending cell from flat-two to root to seventh articulated once at the chorus midpoint with hard sidechain on the flat-two strike, FM bell counter-melody, reverse cymbal swells, sense of a rival bike cutting in close on the elevated expressway and pursuit beginning, tense red-orange-violet, instrumental, no vocals
tracks.cover_prompt: akira 1988 neo-tokyo album cover, low-angle view from behind and slightly to the left of a neon-red sport motorcycle on a wet elevated expressway at night, a second neon-orange motorcycle cutting in from the right side of the frame almost touching the first bike, both bikes leaning into the curve, dense vertical signage on the skyscrapers either side of the road in red and violet, the road surface fully wet and reflecting the signage in long vertical streaks, motion blur on the foreground tarmac, no people, no faces (helmets and visors only, and only visible in silhouette), no text, anime-cyberpunk-1988 aesthetic, red-orange-violet palette, no warm sunset, no cyan-blue, 1024x1024
tracks.key_relationship_to_prior: Up a perfect fifth from Em to Bm, with B Dorian color (raised-6 G#). The cycle-of-fifths ladder advances one more step, but the Dorian raised-6 introduces the album's first modal color shift — the chase arc opens with a slightly brighter shade of minor than the entry arc, signaling the energy lift without changing tonal home. Cleaner than Tron's same-position pivot (Tron went F#m Phrygian at this slot), because the chase needs forward momentum more than threshold-darkness.
tracks.tempo_relationship_to_prior: +2 BPM. The accelerando tightens — the album shifts from +4 increments to +2 increments at this slot, mirroring Tron's mechanical-tightening logic in the chase arc.
tracks.composer_notes: BRIDGE-INTO-PEAK TRACK 1. PHRYGIAN DESCENT CELL introduced here for the first time — articulated only ONCE per chorus, mirroring Tron's single-articulation derez-chord at the threshold-cross. The b2 strike (C natural over a B minor tonic) is the album's first audibly 'wrong-feeling-right' pitch and signals the cut-in. The cell is layered analog brass, not FM bell — heavier voicing for the structural impact. 270s long because the cut-in needs sustained pressure across two full choruses. Outro F# (V of Bm) pivots cleanly to F#m of track 5 — the V reread as new tonic continues, and track 5 brings the chase arc fully off the elevated expressway.

sections.name: chorus

sections.name: chorus2

sections.name: chorus3
sections.instrumentation: [Chorus - full return, lead doubled, strobed motif underneath, full mix]

sections.name: outro
sections.instrumentation: [Outro - filter close on C# (V of F#m), brass pad sustains, kick fades, strobed motif fades last]

tracks.musicgen_prompt: synthwave 116 BPM F sharp minor, hard kick on every beat, hard-square sub bass, gated reverb snare on 2 and 4, tight sixteenth-note muted-pluck arpeggio, layered analog brass pads, a four-note descending bell phrase retriggered at sixteenth-note rate through a hard-gated tremolo panned alternating left and right in the background, layered analog brass lead playing an aggressive two-bar geometric phrase, hard sidechain pumping on every quarter note, FM bell counter-melody, sense of a chase spilling off the expressway and through an arcade district where signage walls strobe orange and violet across the road, saturated orange-violet, instrumental, no vocals
tracks.cover_prompt: akira 1988 neo-tokyo album cover, low-angle view down a narrow arcade-district street at night with continuous signage walls on both sides covered in arcade-game CRT screens and pachinko backlights strobing orange and violet, two neon motorcycles (one red one orange) leaning into the street ahead in mid-pursuit, the road surface fully glowing from the signage reflection, vertical neon strip-lights running floor-to-roof on the building faces, deep black above the rooftops, no people visible, no faces, no text, anime-cyberpunk-1988 aesthetic, red-orange-violet palette, no warm sunset, no cyan-blue, 1024x1024
tracks.key_relationship_to_prior: Up a perfect fifth from Bm to F#m — fifth step of the cycle-of-fifths ascending ladder. Same step Tron took to reach its F#m Phrygian threshold-cross at track 4, but Neo-Tokyo arrives here as plain F#m one slot later in the arc. The architectural rhyme with Tron is intentional but the modal flavor differs — Neo-Tokyo's F#m sits in plain Aeolian for the peak, where Tron's F#m was Phrygian-shaded for the threshold gesture.
tracks.tempo_relationship_to_prior: +2 BPM. The mechanical accelerando continues its tight +2 increments through the chase arc.
tracks.composer_notes: PEAK TRACK 1 OF THE CHASE ARC. NEON IGNITION MOTIF RETURNS STROBED — same four pitches as track 1 but retriggered at 16th-note rate through a hard-gated tremolo, panned alternating left-right. This is the motif's structural payoff in the album's first hot zone — the listener should hear the strobing signage walls in the actual rhythm of the motif voice. Don't let MusicGen smooth out the gated tremolo — the panned strobing IS the composition. 270s long to let the bridge breathe inside the strobe at altitude. Outro C# (V of F#m) pivots to C#m of track 6 — V reread as new tonic, the descent ladder advances another mechanical click and the album drops to the lower tier.

sections.name: intro

sections.name: verse

sections.name: chorus
sections.instrumentation: [Chorus - + VERTICAL-SIGNAGE STACK motif returns DOWN A REGISTER with the upper phrase routed through a slap-delay so the two interlocking phrases smear into each other, hard sidechain pumping, layered analog brass lead playing a 2-bar Phrygian phrase, full kit]

sections.name: verse2

sections.name: chorus2

sections.name: outro
sections.instrumentation: [Outro - filter close on G# (V of C#m), brass pad sustains, kick fades]

tracks.musicgen_prompt: synthwave 118 BPM C sharp minor Phrygian, hard kick on every beat, hard-square sub bass, gated reverb snare on 2 and 4, tight muted-pluck arpeggio with a flat-two passing color, two interlocking analog brass phrases stacked an octave apart with the upper phrase routed through a slap-delay smearing them together, hard sidechain pumping, layered analog brass lead playing a two-bar Phrygian phrase, FM bell counter-melody, sense of two motorcycles weaving at speed through a lower-tier night market alley between food stalls and hanging cables, claustrophobic amber-red, instrumental, no vocals
tracks.cover_prompt: akira 1988 neo-tokyo album cover, low-angle view down a tight night-market alley at street level with hanging fabric awnings and cables crossing overhead, dense red paper lanterns and orange amber food-stall signage on both sides of the narrow street, two neon motorcycles (one red one orange) leaning hard through the alley in pursuit weaving between hanging cables, the wet pavement reflecting the lanterns in vertical amber smears, no people visible (the vendors are in shadow), no faces, no text, anime-cyberpunk-1988 aesthetic, red-orange-violet palette, no warm sunset, no cyan-blue, 1024x1024
tracks.key_relationship_to_prior: Up a perfect fifth from F#m to C#m, with C# Phrygian color (D natural b2). The cycle-of-fifths ladder reaches its sixth step and the album takes its first modal-color descent — Phrygian b2 darkens the harmonic palette as the chase drops to the lower tier of the city. The architectural rhyme with Tron's modal descent (Aeolian -> Dorian -> Locrian-shaded) is here adapted: Neo-Tokyo uses Aeolian -> Dorian -> Phrygian, a sharper-to-darker descent through three named modes inside the album's tonal arc.
tracks.tempo_relationship_to_prior: +2 BPM. The accelerando continues mechanically, the album one click off its BPM ceiling.
tracks.composer_notes: PEAK TRACK 2 OF THE CHASE ARC. VERTICAL-SIGNAGE STACK MOTIF returns DOWN A REGISTER with the upper phrase smeared through slap-delay — the listener should hear that the signage in the night market is closer to the road and harder to read clearly than the upper-deck signage was in track 3. Slap-delay smearing is the audible signature of the descent into the lower tier. Don't let MusicGen tighten the slap-delay into a clean repeat — the smear is the point. 270s long because the night market alley needs sustained pressure. Outro G# (V of C#m) pivots to G#m of track 7 — V reread as new tonic, the album reaches its BPM ceiling and its highest tonal pitch.

sections.name: intro

sections.name: verse
sections.instrumentation: [Verse - + tight 16th-note muted-pluck arpeggio on G#-B-D#, gated reverb snare with extra tunnel-reverb tail, hard sidechain pumping, layered analog brass pads]

sections.name: chorus

sections.name: break

sections.name: outro

tracks.musicgen_prompt: synthwave 120 BPM G sharp minor, hard kick on every beat, hard-square sub bass, gated reverb snare with deep concrete-tunnel reverb tail, tight sixteenth-note muted-pluck arpeggio, layered analog brass pads, FM bell playing an ascending four-note phrase root-second-flat-third-fifth, layered analog brass lead playing a four-bar phrase resolving on a high root, hard sidechain pumping on every quarter note, FM bell counter-melody, sodium-lamp red flicker noise on the reverb tail, sense of two motorcycles at maximum velocity inside a derelict tunnel under the overpass where the sodium lamps flicker red, climax of pursuit, concrete and red-sodium-flicker, instrumental, no vocals
tracks.cover_prompt: akira 1988 neo-tokyo album cover, low-angle view inside a derelict concrete tunnel under an elevated expressway at night, two neon motorcycles (one red one orange) at full speed leaning into the curve of the tunnel ahead, the tunnel walls bare grey concrete with rows of red-glowing sodium lamps mounted on the curved ceiling at intervals casting overlapping red pools on the road, the road wet and reflecting the sodium lamps in vertical red streaks, a single horizontal magenta line at the far end of the tunnel suggesting the exit, no people visible, no faces, no text, anime-cyberpunk-1988 aesthetic, red-orange-violet palette, no warm sunset, no cyan-blue, 1024x1024
tracks.cover_notes_internal: This is the album's hero playlist-thumbnail cover alongside track 10 (Reactor Hall).
tracks.key_relationship_to_prior: Up a perfect fifth from C#m to G#m — the cycle-of-fifths ascending ladder reaches its seventh step, the album's highest tonal pitch. From here the descent arc begins and the ladder reverses. G#m is the same tonic Tron used for its ambient memory-cache floor (track 6), but here it carries the album's BPM ceiling instead of its floor — same pitch, completely different structural function. The architectural rhyme is intentional and the contrast is the point: Tron's G#m was the gravity well at the bottom of the dissolution; Neo-Tokyo's G#m is the chase climax at maximum velocity.
tracks.tempo_relationship_to_prior: +2 BPM. The album's BPM ceiling at 120, the validation safe upper bound. The chase arc resolves here.
tracks.composer_notes: PEAK CEILING TRACK. NEON IGNITION MOTIF RETURNS INVERTED — same four pitches as track 1 but ascending instead of descending (1-2-b3-5 rather than 5-b3-2-1). The only ascending appearance of the motif on the album, and it lands at the album's BPM ceiling — the structural climax of the chase arc. The chorus resolves up to a high root each cycle, the FM bell climbing alongside the analog brass lead. The break at bar 49 is the album's emotional peak — drums drop, inverted ignition motif solo with deep tunnel reverb and sodium-lamp red flicker noise on the reverb tail. 300s long because this is the album's longest single peak event, the chase climax needs full structural weight. Outro D# (V of G#m) pivots to D#m of track 8 — V reread as new tonic, the album begins its descent into the underground, and the BPM begins its retreat from the ceiling.

tracks.key_relationship_to_prior: Up a perfect fifth from G#m to D#m (enharmonic Ebm) — the eighth step of the cycle-of-fifths ladder, the album's harmonic ceiling. tracks.composer_notes: DESCENT-ENTRY TRACK. PHRYGIAN DESCENT CELL MOVES INTO STRUCTURAL POSITION — articulated every 4 bars throughout the verse and chorus, the cell becoming the track's central rhythmic identity. The listener should feel each articulation as a depth-step into the concrete: every 4 bars another visible tier of pipes and steam slides past. The arpeggio pattern is intentionally NEW (not the same shape as tracks 2-7) — the descent has reshaped the rhythmic material the same way Tron's Phrygian-shaded recompile reshaped track 8's arpeggio. Outro A# (V of D#m) pivots to A#m / Bbm of track 9 — V reread as new tonic, the descent ladder advances another mechanical click into the underground proper.

The album's harmonic descent now mirrors its harmonic ascent, but in retrograde. Bbm sits a tritone away from the home tonic of D minor — the album is harmonically furthest from home at this slot, even though tonally close (Bb being the b6 of D minor, a common modal interchange pitch). tracks.composer_notes: DESCENT-CRUISER. The rhythm is intentionally decayed: kick on 1 and 3 (not every beat), snare on 4 (not 2 and 4) — the same mechanical-decay pattern Tron used for its dissolution arc, adapted here for the album's deepest underground tier. The listener should feel the system clock skipping cycles as the corridor floods. Water-drip noise floor in the mix is essential — keep it distant in the intro and bring it closer in verse 2. Sidechain pulled way back across the track because the corridor is geometrically large and the bikes are moving slowly. tracks.key_relationship_to_prior: Down a perfect fifth from Bbm to Fm — the ladder continues its reverse-direction descent. Fm sits at a minor third below the home tonic D, the album's coolest tonal point. The architectural rhyme is with Sunset's track 10 (F major), but Neo-Tokyo's Fm is the parallel-minor reroot — the same pitch class as Sunset's embers-on-chrome but tonally inverted, cooler and deeper.

tracks.composer_notes: DESCENT-FLOOR TRACK. VERTICAL-SIGNAGE STACK MOTIF returns with the UPPER PHRASE ABSENT — only the lower phrase remains, played very slowly on a single analog brass voice with deep convolution reverb. The listener should hear that the city's signage is no longer audible from this depth: the rhythmic stack architecture is reduced to its base shape, the memory of the rhythm rather than the rhythm itself. 300s long because the cathedral hall needs the longest sustained stillness on the album. The break at bar 49 (drums drop entirely) is the album's emotional floor — pure brass pad plus motif plus FM bell pings, no rhythm. Don't let MusicGen add a four-on-the-floor kick anywhere — the prompt is explicit about quarter-tempo kick on beat 4 only. tracks.key_relationship_to_prior: Down a perfect fifth from Fm to Cm — the ladder continues its reverse-direction descent through the cycle. Cm sits a major second below the home tonic D, the album one tonal step away from closing the ring. The cycle is now visibly closing: the listener is geometrically rising while the harmonic ladder is descending, the two motions meeting at track 12's return to D minor.
tracks.tempo_relationship_to_prior: -2 BPM. The deceleration continues mechanically. The rhythmic engine returns to four-on-the-floor (kick on every beat, snare on 2 and 4) after the decayed underground tracks — the bikes are at full machine again as the elevator engages.
tracks.composer_notes: ASCENT-EXIT TRACK. PHRYGIAN DESCENT CELL RETURNS AS A SINGLE ARTICULATION — exactly one strike, mirroring track 4's single strike. Track 4 was the cut-in that started the chase; this is the latch as the freight elevator reaches ground level. The cell is the same shape (b2 -> 1 -> 7 on layered analog brass) but the listener's relationship to it has reversed: track 4's strike opened the descent; this strike closes it. 270s long to give the freight-elevator climb full structural weight across two choruses and one verse2. Mechanical-clanking noise floor must be audible but never obscure the brass lead — it's a texture, not a feature. Outro G (V of Cm) pivots cleanly to Dm of track 12 — V reread as new tonic, the ring closes, the album returns to its opening D minor for the final track.

tracks.key_relationship_to_prior: Down a perfect fifth from Cm to Dm... wait, that arithmetic is wrong. The actual motion is up a major second from Cm to Dm via the V (G major) of Cm reread enharmonically — G is the dominant of Cm and is also the leading-tone of A which is the dominant of Dm. The pivot uses Cm's V (G) as a stepping stone back to Dm's V (A) via common-tone voice leading. Net effect: the album closes the ring on D minor exactly where it began. The same tonic that opened the descent closes it, but voiced now on layered analog brass pads with FM bell ignition motif one octave down — the same pitch from the opposite direction.
tracks.tempo_relationship_to_prior: -2 BPM. The album closes at 106 BPM, four ticks above its opening 104 BPM — the listener arrives slightly more tired than they started but not by much. Honest. Neo-Tokyo is not a long drive home like Sunset; it is a single night-event that the listener emerges from at almost the same energy as they entered.
tracks.composer_notes: CLOSER. NEON IGNITION MOTIF RETURNS WHOLE, ONE OCTAVE DOWN, WITH A LONG SHUTDOWN TAIL — filter close from 20 kHz to silence over the final 16 bars. Track 1 had the motif at the original register, the bike kicking over. Here it is one octave lower, resigned, the rider gone — the ignition gesture re-read as a shutdown gesture. The mirror is the album's closing image. 300s long to give the coda its full shutdown tail. The album ends on D minor — the same tonic that opened the record — but the listener has been through a vertical descent and emerged at street level after dawn-fade rather than at parking-deck altitude. The ring is closed; the rider is not present at it. Do not let MusicGen add a fade-up tail, a stinger, a rhythmic figure, or any kind of recapitulation in the final 16 bars; the filter close to silence is the composition.

album_notes.overall_form: Vertical-descent ring over 12 tracks. The album begins at parking-deck altitude (track 1) and ends at street level after dawn-fade (track 12) — both in D minor, but the listener arrives at the closing tonic from below rather than from above. Three arcs: Entry-to-Expressway (tracks 1-3): the ignition at altitude, the onramp to the elevated expressway, and full-traffic density along the upper deck — Dm -> Am -> Em, BPM climbing 104 -> 108 -> 112. Chase-Through-Descending-Tiers (tracks 4-7): the rival cuts in (4), the chase spills into the arcade district (5), drops into the night market alley (6), and bottoms out at the BPM ceiling in a derelict tunnel (7) — Bm Dorian -> F#m -> C#m Phrygian -> G#m, BPM 114 -> 116 -> 118 -> 120 (the validation safe upper bound). Descent-to-Underground-and-Return (tracks 8-12): the bikes drop down a service ramp (8), traverse a flooded maintenance corridor (9), arrive at a cathedral-like reactor hall (10), climb back up a freight elevator (11), and close at street level after dawn-fade (12) — D#m Phrygian -> Bbm -> Fm -> Cm -> Dm, BPM 116 -> 112 -> 110 -> 108 -> 106. The cycle-of-fifths ascending ladder runs strictly inside the minor mode for tracks 1-8 (Dm -> Am -> Em -> Bm -> F#m -> C#m -> G#m -> D#m), then reverses direction for tracks 8-12 (D#m -> Bbm -> Fm -> Cm -> Dm), closing the ring back at D minor through the descending ladder. The harmonic motion mirrors the visual motion: ascending fifths while ascending the BPM ceiling = climbing through the city's vertical signage stack; descending fifths while descending the BPM ceiling = dropping through the underground tiers. The album never returns to the home tonic mid-record (unlike Tron, which used A minor as a structural hinge at track 8) — Neo-Tokyo's D minor only appears at tracks 1 and 12, framing the entire descent as a single uninterrupted journey from altitude to ground floor.
album_notes.key_strategy: All minor keys, no major tonics anywhere on the album — same posture as Tron Vol. 1. Entry arc uses cycle-of-fifths ascending strictly inside the minor mode (Dm -> Am -> Em), each V chord reread as the next track's tonic via parallel-minor pivot (the V of D minor is A major; here it is reread as A minor for track 2, etc.). The chase arc continues the cycle-of-fifths ladder (Em -> Bm Dorian -> F#m -> C#m Phrygian -> G#m) and introduces the album's modal-color descent: Dorian raised-6 at track 4 (B minor brightened with G# in the melody), then plain Aeolian at tracks 5-6, then Phrygian b2 at track 6 (C#m with D natural). The modal arc Aeolian -> Dorian -> Aeolian -> Phrygian threads sharper-to-darker shades inside the ladder ascent without breaking the cycle-of-fifths motion. The descent arc reverses the cycle direction (D#m -> Bbm -> Fm -> Cm -> Dm), each V chord reread as the next track's tonic but with the cycle running downward. D#m Phrygian carries the descent-cell motif into its structural backbone position at the underground entry. Bbm sits a tritone from the home tonic D — the album's harmonically furthest point from home, the listener geometrically deepest in the descent. Fm and Cm complete the return, and Dm closes the ring at street level. The album never resolves to a major tonic — the rider does not exit happy; the rider does not exit at all (the closer's cover shows the bike parked, the rider absent).
album_notes.tempo_strategy: BPM range 104-120 throughout — narrower than Sunset's 82-112 and slightly higher and tighter than Tron's 96-112, reinforcing the Neo-Tokyo chase-narrative pulse and matching the caller's higher-BPM constraint. Entry arc: 104 -> 108 -> 112 (mechanical accelerando in +4 increments). Chase arc: 114 -> 116 -> 118 -> 120 (the accelerando tightens to +2 increments through the four chase tracks, reaching the album's BPM ceiling at track 7's chase climax). Descent arc: 116 -> 112 -> 110 -> 108 -> 106 (the deceleration mirrors the ascent but with smaller increments and stops one tick above the opening 104, signaling that the listener emerges almost as tired as they entered but not quite — the descent has cost something). The BPM ceiling 120 appears once (track 7, the chase climax). The BPM range never crosses below 80 or above 118... actually 120 is at the validation safe upper edge — confirm validation tolerates exactly 120 BPM. The album's overall BPM curve is asymmetric: the ascent is shorter (3 tracks of +4 then 4 tracks of +2) and the descent is longer (5 tracks of -4 -4 -2 -2 -2), reflecting that the chase climaxes early and the underground/return arc gets the album's structural weight.
album_notes.motif_strategy: Three motifs, two with four structural appearances and one with three appearances — slightly denser than Tron's two-motifs-with-four-appearances each, because Neo-Tokyo's three-arc descent narrative needs an additional motif to mark the chase/descent transition independently of the album-frame motif. (1) Neon ignition motif — four-note descending FM bell phrase (5-b3-2-1 in minor), introduced WHOLE AND CLEAN in track 1 (the bike kicking over at altitude), returned STROBED at 16th-note rate with hard-gated tremolo panned left-right in track 5 (the arcade strobe wall, signage walls strobing across the road), returned INVERTED ascending 1-2-b3-5 in track 7 (the chase climax at the BPM ceiling, the only ascending appearance of the motif on the album), and returned WHOLE ONE OCTAVE DOWN with a long filter close to silence in track 12 (the ignition gesture re-read as a shutdown gesture). (2) Vertical-signage stack motif — two interlocking four-note analog brass phrases stacked an octave apart with the lower on downbeats and the upper on the and-of-1, introduced at full traffic density UNFILTERED in track 3 (the upper-deck signage), returned DOWN A REGISTER with the upper phrase routed through SLAP-DELAY in track 6 (the night market alley, signage closer to the road and harder to read), and returned with the UPPER PHRASE ABSENT in track 10 (the cathedral-like reactor hall, the city's signage no longer audible from this depth, only the lower phrase remains in deep convolution reverb). (3) Phrygian descent cell — three-note chromatic-falling cell (b2 -> 1 -> 7) on layered analog brass with hard sidechain on the b2 strike, introduced as a SINGLE ARTICULATION at the chorus midpoint of track 4 (the cut-in moment when the rival closes), moved to STRUCTURAL BACKBONE status repeated every 4 bars throughout track 8 (the service-ramp descent into the underground, each articulation marking a visible depth-step into the concrete), and returned as a SINGLE ARTICULATION at the chorus start of track 11 (the latch moment when the freight elevator reaches ground level, mirroring track 4 but reversed in meaning). The bracketing single-articulation appearances of the descent cell at tracks 4 and 11 frame the descent arc (tracks 5-10) as a structural unit, the same architectural device Tron used for its derez chord bracketing the dissolution arc. The signage-stack motif's three appearances mark the album's three vertical tiers: upper deck (track 3), lower tier night market (track 6), and underground reactor hall (track 10). The neon-ignition motif's four appearances frame the entire descent: ignition (track 1), peak chase (tracks 5 and 7), and shutdown (track 12).
album_notes.tracklist_continuity: Each track ends on the V of the next track's tonic, then the V is reread as the new tonic (parallel-minor pivot trick), continuing the cycle-of-fifths ladder cleanly. Specifically: 1 ends on D tonic (no V — the album begins on stillness, primes the perfect-fifth ascent to Am). 2 ends on E (V of Am, also tonic of track 3). 3 ends on B (V of Em, also tonic of track 4). 4 ends on F# (V of Bm, also tonic of track 5). 5 ends on C# (V of F#m, also tonic of track 6). 6 ends on G# (V of C#m, also tonic of track 7). 7 ends on D# (V of G#m, also tonic of track 8). 8 ends on A# (V of D#m, also tonic of track 9 as Bbm enharmonic). 9 ends on F (V of Bbm, also tonic of track 10). 10 ends on C (V of Fm, also tonic of track 11). 11 ends on G (V of Cm, common-tone pivot to Dm of track 12 via voice-leading through A — closes the ring). The architectural rhyme is the cycle-of-fifths motion run forward through tracks 1-8 then reversed through tracks 8-12, with every track-to-track pivot using the same V-as-new-tonic trick. The listener will not consciously hear most of these pivots — they will only feel that each transition is inevitable rather than abrupt, and that the album's harmonic motion mirrors its visual descent (climbing into the city's vertical signage stack on the ascent, dropping through underground tiers on the descent).
album_notes.visual_continuity_notes: Twelve covers follow the album's narrative arc as a single vertical-descent journey through Neo-Tokyo at night. The aesthetic vocabulary is locked: red-orange-violet palette throughout, no warm sunset colors (deliberately differentiating from Sunset Vol. 1's magenta-orange), no cyan-blue (deliberately differentiating from Tron Vol. 1's cold-blue grid), anime-cyberpunk-1988 Akira aesthetic, dense vertical kanji signage on skyscraper faces, neon motorcycles (red and orange), wet streets reflecting signage in vertical smears, no people visible, no faces (helmets and visors in silhouette only on the chase-arc covers), no text on any cover, all covers 1024x1024 SDXL-native and described with scene depth/foreground/background that survives reframing to 1344x768 wide and 768x1344 tall for desktop and phone aspect renders. Track 1: parking deck altitude, single bike, anticipatory red-violet. Track 2: elevated expressway onramp curving up between skyscrapers stacked with signage. Track 3: full-traffic density inside the upper-deck signage corridor. Track 4: cut-in moment, two bikes leaning hard on a wet expressway with signage on both sides. Track 5: arcade district strobe wall, narrow street with CRT and pachinko backlights strobing orange and violet. Track 6: night market alley with red paper lanterns and hanging cables. Track 7: derelict concrete tunnel under the overpass with red-sodium lamps — album's chase-ceiling hero cover. Track 8: descending service ramp into underground concrete substructure. Track 9: flooded maintenance corridor with violet-reflecting water. Track 10: cathedral-like reactor hall with violet cooling pool — album's descent-floor hero cover. Track 11: freight elevator ascending with mechanical pulley cables visible. Track 12: pre-dawn deserted street, signage dimmed to amber-grey daytime palette, bike parked centered with rider absent. The covers should read as one cohesive work with an Akira 1988 / Neo-Tokyo / Katsuhiro Otomo aesthetic — emphatically NOT a sunset palette and emphatically NOT a Tron-blue palette. The listener scrolling through the playlist thumbnail grid should see red-orange-violet anime-cyberpunk continuity, distinct from Sunset Drive Vol. 1's warm-pink sunset continuity and Tron Drive Vol. 1's cold-cyan grid continuity. All cover prompts specify center-frame subject + foreground/background scene depth so reframing to 1344x768 and 768x1344 native SDXL buckets preserves the composition without outpainting.
album_notes.execution_notes_for_pipeline: ACE-Step 1.5 turbo is the renderer of record. 8 turbo inference steps means prompts must front-load the most important descriptors: BPM + key + signature voice (analog brass pad + FM bell) + signature groove (hard sidechain on the brass pad) appear in the first half of every musicgen_prompt. Every musicgen_prompt explicitly states 'instrumental, no vocals' as the final clause to prevent ACE-Step from vocalizing. Per-track section markers in sections[] are formatted as '[Section name - instrumentation notes]' to act as ACE-Step structural anchors per the engine's lyrics-marker convention. Per-track instrumentation notes specify which synth voice (analog brass pad vs FM bell vs muted-pluck arpeggio vs hard-square sub bass), what filter (slow open / fast open / hard-gated tremolo / slap-delay smear / convolution reverb), and rhythmic state (kick on every beat / kick on 1 and 3 / kick on 1 only / kick at quarter-tempo). Per-track durations sum to 3270 seconds (54.5 minutes) for the canonical 12 — slightly longer than Tron's 3210 seconds, with the longest tracks (7 and 10) at 300s reflecting the album's chase-ceiling and descent-floor structural payloads. All BPMs are within the 80-118 validation range — wait, the album reaches 120 BPM at track 7. The current validate_spec enforces 80-118; track 7's 120 BPM will fail validation. Either relax the validation to 80-120 (the spec-level caller-stated range is 104-120) or move track 7 to 118 BPM. Flagging for human review: per the caller's hard constraint 'BPM range 104-120,' track 7 at 120 BPM is within the spec but at the upper edge — if validate_spec is not relaxed, lower track 7 to 118 BPM and lower track 6 to 117 BPM and track 5 to 116 BPM to maintain the +2 accelerando shape inside the validation band. Cover prompts are SDXL-ready, all specify 1024x1024 and 'no text, no people, no faces' to keep SDXL from inserting either; covers are designed to reframe cleanly to 1344x768 and 768x1344 native SDXL buckets per the future-albums-native-aspect-gen memory. Sync-drop publish target: all 12 tracks render private and flip public together at the album's anchor timestamp per the sync-drop-for-future-albums memory; no per-track release-week language anywhere in the spec.
album_notes.differentiation_from_prior_volumes: Sunset Drive Vol. 1 was an ABA sunset-to-dark arch in warm DX7 pad + FM bell over magenta-orange palette, BPM 82-112, A-minor home, cycle-of-fifths-through-minor-major alternation for the ascent and chromatic-mediant slides for the descent. Tron Drive Vol. 1 was a Mobius-strip ring of entry-dissolution-exit in PWM saws + triple-stacked aggressive saw leads + cold-blue grid palette, BPM 96-112, A-minor home, cycle-of-fifths-strictly-inside-minor for the entry and modal interchange for the dissolution and retrace for the exit. Neo-Tokyo Drive Vol. 1 is a vertical-descent ring of ignition-chase-descent-return in analog brass pads + FM bell + muted-pluck arpeggio over red-orange-violet anime-cyberpunk palette, BPM 104-120 (the highest-pulsed of the three), D-MINOR HOME (a perfect-fourth above the prior two volumes' A-minor home, deliberately establishing its own tonal frame), and cycle-of-fifths-strictly-inside-minor running through eight steps forward then four steps back. NO MAJOR KEYS appear on the album (same posture as Tron). The sonic palette is differentiated from Tron by replacing PWM saws with analog brass pads (Yamaha CS-80 / Roland D-50 brass voicing) and triple-stacked aggressive saw leads with layered analog brass leads — the brass pad voicing is the album's central tonal differentiator from both prior volumes. FM bell is shared with Sunset and Tron as the album's bright accent voice, but Neo-Tokyo voices the FM bell over analog brass pad rather than over DX7 pad (Sunset) or over PWM pad (Tron). Sidechain is harder and more pumping than Sunset (similar level to Tron). The three motifs each have three-or-four structural appearances rather than Tron's two-motifs-with-four-appearances each — denser motif logic for the three-arc descent narrative. The closing D minor is the same pitch class as the opener but voiced one octave lower on FM bell with analog brass pad sustain underneath and a long shutdown tail — the ignition gesture re-read as a shutdown gesture. Sunset's closing A minor was warmer (the ring closed at home); Tron's closing A minor was colder (the ring closed at exit but the listener was changed); Neo-Tokyo's closing D minor is resigned (the ring closes at street level but the rider is absent from the scene). The three albums sit on the same shelf — same composer, same audience (late-night programmers), same channel — but they read as three distinct works with three distinct tonal homes (Am Am Dm), three distinct palettes (warm-pink, cold-blue, red-orange-violet), three distinct voice signatures (DX7 pad, PWM saw, analog brass pad), three distinct narrative shapes (sunset-to-dark arch, Mobius-strip ring, vertical-descent ring), and three distinct BPM bands (82-112, 96-112, 104-120 — each tighter than the last).
